title: Annex — A match's life cycle: lobby, personas & reconnection
meta.desc: How a Tower Duel match is set up, played out and replayed: the phone's 4 views, the Architect/Rival personas, the state machine and reconnection — shown through four interactive simulators.
hero.title: A match's life cycle
hero.lead: How a match is set up, played out and replayed — on the phones and on the TV. Personas, views, state machine and reconnection, all through four hands-on simulators.
card.title: Match life cycle
card.desc: Lobby, Architect/Rival personas, state machine and reconnection — with 4 interactive simulators.
d.h2: Discovery — the 4 views and the 2 personas
d.lead: On every phone exactly one view is shown at a time, and the journey is always the same.
d.p1: Four canonical views follow one another:
d.li.accueil: Home — type the room code and a handle, then join.
d.li.lobby: Lobby — set the match up (page 1) and pick your side (page 2).
d.li.command: Command — aim and fire, round after round.
d.p2: Two roles share the setup. The Architect (first to connect, or the loser on a rematch) configures the battlefield on page 1 and triggers the start by validating it. The Rival has no access to page 1, but can rush to page 2 and claim a side — fastest wins. The match starts once page 1 is validated, a side is claimed, and both players are present.
sim2.title: When does the match start?
sim2.hint: Tick the three conditions: the start only fires with all three met. Page 1 belongs to the Architect; the side, though, can be claimed by anyone.
i.p1: The displayed screen follows from a single current state. Walk the token through the machine: from each state, only the allowed transitions are offered. You find the cycle Lobby → Command → Cracktro, then “Replay” (back to the lobby) or “Quit” (back home).
sim3.title: State-machine walker
sim3.hint: Tap a transition to advance. The phone screen (centre) always reflects the single current view.
i.tip: Takeaway. A single field drives the display: it is impossible to show two views at once. That is the “freezing” of the state machine.
e.p1: On first connect, every device (player and TV) negotiates a unique token. If it loses the network, the server holds its place for 10 s: by reconnecting in time with its token, it reclaims exactly its seat. Beyond that the seat is freed — and if it is the TV (the room's anchor) that vanishes, the whole match is closed.
sim4.title: 10 s grace window
sim4.hint: Slide the reconnect moment. Before 10 s: the seat is reclaimed. After: seat lost (player) or room destroyed (TV).
e.p2: Another polished detail: the transition between rounds. The camera slides horizontally toward the next round, in parallax (the far scenery slides slower). The trap: when the camera resets, the scenery “jumps” back. The fix is to bake the shift into the scenery so it keeps flowing seamlessly.
sim1.title: Scenery continuity (parallax)
sim1.hint: With Bake: the world flows on, no jump. Without Bake: on the camera reset the scenery snaps back — the bug we fix. The golden tower is the fixed reference.
e.nerd: For the curious. The sky doesn't move (factor 0), the mountains slide at 25–45 %, the sun at 12 %. “Baking” means re-applying that offset after the camera reset: the parallax world then scrolls on continuously, round after round.
